system_prompt:
You are extracting load-bearing passages from one chapter of a guitar method book.

The user will give you the verbatim text of one chapter. Extract every passage
that does any of the following:

- teaches a technique (how to play, fingering, hand position, picking pattern)
- defines a concept (a chord type, a scale, a position system, a terminology)
- gives a rule (when to do X, what to avoid, what to prefer)
- shows a load-bearing example (an exercise that illustrates a method's
  characteristic move)

Output a JSON object with this shape:

  { "quotes": [
      { "topic": "<short topic label>",
        "verbatim_quote": "<exact substring of the input>",
        "why_load_bearing": "<one sentence on why this passage matters>",
        "page": <int, optional>
      },
      ...
  ] }

CRITICAL RULES:
1. `verbatim_quote` MUST be an EXACT substring of the input text. Do not
   paraphrase. Do not add words. Do not fix typos. Copy character-for-character.
2. If you can't find a substantial passage in the input that fits a topic,
   skip the topic — don't fabricate.
3. Skip prose that is purely biographical, motivational, or transitional.
   Keep prose that teaches.
4. Aim for 3-15 quotes per chapter. A chapter with only one load-bearing
   passage gets one quote; a dense chapter gets many.
5. `topic` is a short label (2-6 words). Use the book's own terminology
   when possible.
6. `why_load_bearing` is one sentence in YOUR voice explaining the
   significance — not a paraphrase of the quote.

Chapter 10: Scales, Modes, Soloing Strategies (Lessons 59-70)
Pages 121-144 of the book.

MB SCALE POSITIONS AND
iin BROAD SEQUENCES

| took a class of music composition at Santa Monica Junior College, and the first thing the teacher got into was sequencing
lines or melodies. By using fingering positions on the fretboard, we can put sequences together if we familiarize ourselves
with where these notes are. It takes a great deal of practice, but the reward is being able to construct lines quickly, which is
essential in order to be a competent jazz musician. These sequences are built from two positions of the C major scale.

in the same key.

LESSON #61:

This chord progression is very similar to many jazz standards and should provide you with an adequate accompaniment
for practicing your improvisations over chord changes. To simplify our approach in playing over so many chords, we should
analyze any tune to see how chords can be grouped into keys. The purpose of this is to demonstrate that we only need to
apply one scale to groups of chords that are related to one key. There are many approaches or angles you can take, but the
basic principles in this lesson are important to learn for any jazz student.

The key of F major is Fmaj7, Gm7, Am7, Bsmaj7, C7, Dm7, and Em7+5. Of the first eleven chords in the previous progression,
only two are not in the key of F major: Bbm7 and G#m7. Since those outside-the-key chords come and go quickly, you can
choose to not pay attention to them. In that case, you can solo over the first section using just the F major scale. When these
non-diatonic chords (Bbm7 and Gim7) pop up, you can emphasize their chord tones by adding them to your melodies. The
same progression is transposed up a half step to F# major in measure 9 and again another half step to G major in measure 17.
At the end of the form, in measure 24, a ii-V (Gm7-C7) leads us back to the home key of F for the repeat. These transpositions
by half step are a common occurrence in jazz.

This lesson of playing over chord changes was designed to teach you a concept. My intention was not to show you the
melodies to use in the future, but inspire you to work at making up your own using this same system. If you like what is
contained within these lines, then by all means incorporate them into your style, but please try to venture ahead using your
own creative gifts. Jazz calls for your own spontaneous expression.

I've tried to make it obvious that the melodies from the scale directly relate to the chords. This is about as conservative as you
get from me, but | like it just as much as jazz that can go outside the obvious theories. When you study jazz standards, you
discover how musicians/composers harmonize their scales with the chords. Try to do this by writing out some of your own
chord progressions with the harmonizing scale/melodies.

100 JAZZ LESSONS | GUITAR LESSON GOLDMINE 125



ale) EF i-y+ag PENTATONICS IN DIATONICS

The purpose of this study is to expand the possibilities of playing scales in different ways. The C diatonic major scale (C-D-E-F-G-
A-8) contains three minor pentatonic scales within it: A minor, D minor, and E minor. If you include the 11th or 45th note (Fé or G
respectively), which is a common and useful sound in jazz, you can use the B minor pentatonic scale as well. This gives you four
different pentatonic scales that are built from the C major scale. My approach to jazz is to try and break away from the regular scale
fingerings and not let it sound as though you're playing a certain scale at all. Many students practice scales religiously, but trap
themselves by never doing more than just that. Discovering that these pentatonic notes fall within the C major scale is a great way of
learning how to construct melodies for jazz. In this lesson, I've only charted out the scale positions. Putting them to use is the subject
of another lesson. The Roman numerals name the scale position/pattern by the degree of the scale with which it begins.

Here’s a summary of what we've seen:

C diatonic major scale = C-D-E-F-G-A-B: This scale has no sharps or flats and is the white keys of the piano.
A minor pentatonic scale = A-C-D-E-G: These notes are contained in the C major scale.

D minor pentatonic scale = D-F-G-A-C: These notes are contained in the C major scale as well.

E minor pentatonic scale = E~-G-A-B-D: These notes are contained in the C major scale as well.

I've included the B minor pentatonic scale, because the use of the #11th or »5th (Fé or G.) is common in jazz.
B minor pentatonic scale = B~-D-E-F#-A: All but one note (Fé) is found in the C major scale.

Most teachers begin showing students the pentatonic scales because they're popular in rock and blues. We can take
advantage of that and apply that knowledge to jazz. Experiment with these sounds over a Cmaj7 chord.

MB REPEATING RIFF

Playing a riff or phrase and then repeating it in a new position is what we're covering in this lesson. | don't like to soumm
predictable when | play, but it's common to hear this concept being used in jazz. One guitarist that comes to mind is Jom
Abercrombie. | saw him with Billy Cobham’'s band, and he ripped some very fast fusion riffs. About the only thing | could i®
up from his style was this riff-shifting idea. He was quite an inspiration. He did some nice acoustic stuff too. This first examen
goes from Gm7 up a minor 3rd to Bsm7. This is a common shift you hear often.

This melody is based on a i-iv-v progression: Gm-Cm-Dm. There's plenty of reason to practice this type of sound. Many
popular standards and the blues have these same sorts of chord changes.

Every chord change can be a scale change. There are many things you can practice without having a song in front of you
in written sheet music. Pick out a few chords that are in different keys and practice soloing back and forth over them. Pat
Metheny said it took him five years of hard work to be able to take off in any harmonic direction. If you're studying to be 2
jazz musician, be prepared to go the distance. This is a high art form and not a “gimme.” The personal satisfaction from this
music is greater than being a one hit wonder (though that money would be nice!). I've met musicians that have been looking
for a hit their whole life, but can’t play jazz too well. Jazz takes dedication and a lifetime's worth of commitment.

100 JAZZ LESSONS | GUITAR LESSON GOLDMINE 131



SOLOING MAJOR TO MINOR

A common but beautiful sound in jazz is going from a major chord to a minor one built off the same root. One convenient
habit many guitarists have is to move a major scale up three frets (or a minor 3rd), which turns the sound into the minor of
that same key. I've used that concept here and there before, but in this lesson I've tried to vivify the point of going from major
to minor to help you master it. It's not even obvious that I've shifted in some of these examples, and that’s the point—trying
not to sound predictable. This makes the sound.of your music more attractive. It’s more fun for the audience if they don't
know what you're going to do next.

EXAMPLE 1

TRACK 15

132 100 JAZZ LESSONS | GUITAR LESSON GOLDMINE


Scenario: Some friends of yours have come to the club where you're playing. It’s your first night at this particular spot, and
in order to impress the club owner, you've invited as many friends and relatives to the gig as you could. As it turns out, the
only people that show up are some of the few friends you've invited, but they brought some people along with them to
try and help you spread the word about your gig. At first, everyone is delighted by your ability, and it looks as though this
venue could turn out to be a real success for you. However, midway through the first set, suddenly everyone at the table in
front of you, which of course is practically everyone there whom all are your friends, stand up and leave. Because youre in
the middle of a tune, you can't find out why they've left, and before you know it the place is empty. At the end of the night,
the bartender is moaning to the boss, and what appeared to be a good thing in the beginning now looks like a short-lived
sad scenario. The next day you call your family and friends and ask them why they left so early. And as it turns out, they all
have the same complaint: you drove them crazy by playing the same lines with the same concepts over and over! Your best
friend's girlfriend, who happened to be a student of music at a local college said, “Your friend can play fast with a lot of good
technique, but he doesn't play those passages with very much taste. He kept playing that same major-to-minor thing over
and over the same way every time he came to it”

Hey, I'm just trying to help!

In this example, I've just shifted the whole line up three frets, or a minor 3rd interval. You can do this whenever you want to,
but as I've said, if you do it too much people will begin to predict what you're going to do next, and that's when they're going
to start getting bored with your playing.

sounds with fluidity and not rely on one or two tricks. When | play nowadays, | see where the two scales are simultaneously
and | can quickly change back and forth from wherever I’m at on the fretboard. In this last example, I'm using an arpeggio.
| go from the tonic major seventh arpeggio (1-3-5-7) of the key to the tonic minor seventh arpeggio (1+3-5-7). These are
basic theories everyone should know if planning on being a jazz musician.

sounds over one dominant chord—in this case, we'll use A7. | call the effect of the minor pentatonic bluesy and the Mixolydian
jazzy. It's good to have options, and the goal is to be able to switch scales in the middle of a passage without a chord change.

Example: Moving down chromatically in half steps (one fret after another) using diminished scales. This is a small taste of

EXAMPLE 5

Learning how to solo over chord changes is important if you want to handle the music in a lot of traditional jazz tunes or
standards. To me, this is the difference between a jazz musician and a pop guitarist. There are loads of jazz players that have
trouble with this aspect, and a great deal of the current music doesn’t have difficult chord changes to negotiate. There's more
emphasis on funky rhythms and rhythmic grooves than on tackling harmonic passages. If you want to broaden your abilities,
the following course in practical jazz chord changes will at least give you some concepts to consider,

| feel the first thing is not to try and play tunes or jazz changes already written by someone else. Try and study some changes
that you make up yourself. The reason for this is that we tend to be more engrossed when we feel we are discovering this
art on our own. Another reason for this is that most students are overwhelmed when they first try and play changes. It can
be very difficult and discouraging. The widespread possibilities seem endless, but we're able to get a grip and better insight
when we see this subject hopefully from this next instruction and explanation.

Lay out an interval sheet in front of you. This could be of your own design, but here’s how | do it:

1, Half step up or down (one fret up or down) (e.g., C to Cz, or C to B)

. Whole step up or down (two frets up or down) (e,g., C to D, or C to Bs)

. Minor 3rd up or down (three frets up or down) (e.g., C to E, or C to A)

. Major 3rd up or down (four frets up or down) (e.g., C to E, or C to Ab)

. Up a 4th (five frets up) (C to F)

. Up a#4th or absth (six frets up) (C to F#/G.) (also known as a tritone: three whole steps)
. Up a 5th (seven frets up) (C to G)

. Up a25th from C to A, which is the same as going down two whole steps as in 24

po ON DY BPW DN

. Up a 6th from C to A, which is the same as going down a minor 3rd as in#3

-_
o

. Up ab7th from C to Bs, which is the same as going down a whole step as in #2
11. Up a major (or natural) 7th from C to B, which is the same as going down a half step as in#1

Practice playing scales between these intervals. For example, play any type of scale, such as A major, and then go down a
whole step to G and play G minor pentatonic. | mean any type of scale. Try moving the same scale up and down the fretboard
the way you move barre chords. You have to get used to shifting the tonal center or root around. Play a C dominant 47th
scale (Mixolydian) and then go up a}5th to G: Mixolydian right after that. If you're a blues player, play in A and then goupa
4th and play in D. Work the sounds of the different keys around. | wouldn't advise trying to implement this in a tune yet. Just
force yourself to play random changes, and eventually you'll get used to the sound of melodies shifting through different
keys or changes. Treat scales and melodies the same way you treat shifting barre chords. Move your melodies around the
fretboard to different positions, and eventually you'll get the hang of playing over changes.

Ble) Ei}: RESOLUTIONS TO THE 5TH

In music, and especially in jazz, there’s a constant experiment and search going on in regard to resolutions. The musician
and composer are trying to find a way to finish an idea or musical phrase and make it sound complete, correct, intelligent
and hopefully, most of all, beautiful. In this lesson, | chose the dominant 5th note (D) of the key of G (G-A-B-C-D-E-F#) as
my target of resolution. Targeting notes and areas of sound is a skill and challenge that all musicians face. Your intuition will

lead you, and the more you experiment, the better you should get at it. Listen closely and familiarize yourself with these
resolutions. There are countless ways to do it.

Frankly, we are just scratching the surface. When we look at a classical composition that might contain hundreds of melodies
or passages, you have to consider that almost the entire piece is trying to resolve itself one way or another. We ourselves have
to determine when to finish a phrase. The question of taste and clarity come into play in a big way. People are known for the
way they do things, and some composers have a knack for beautiful resolutions.

The following are three extended G Aeolian minor phrases. | hope you like the simple resolutions.

EXAMPLE 1
TRACK 18

2 Moderately

Eventually, no matter what type of line or scale you play or use, you'll have to focus on resolving your ideas and trying to
make musical sense to yourself and, most importantly, the listener. The more outside or contemporary your sound becomes,
the more you'll be challenged to make it sound right. Always look to the way we resolve phrases in future lessons during your

jazz studies.

100 JAZZ LESSONS | GUITAR LESSON GOLDMINE 139



LESSON #69:

CHANGE DOWN, GO UP

This lesson is designed to help you see options. When | think of jazz, | want to approach playing melodies with as many choices
as | can come up with. When giving a guitar lesson to an intermediate student, usually the first thing they do when playing
a chord change is to follow the chords around. In other words, when the chord change goes up, the scale goes up; when the
chord change goes down, the scale goes down, etc. There’s nothing wrong with doing that sometimes, but you shouldn't do
it all the time because your playing will become predictable. So in these examples, | have the chord changes going down and
the melodies going up. | start the melody off in one area, and then when the chord change moves down, | move to a higher
position on the fretboard to play in that new key. For my ears, this creates a beautiful melodic effect. A popular jazz tune is
“Little Sunflower, which was performed by the late, great Freddie Hubbard. This tune has a major chord change that goes up
and down a half step, or one fret (B» major to D major). It's a chord change everyone should work on.

EXAMPLE 1

Si, TRACK 19

co2

Moderately

47

Another chord change worth looking at is minor chords moving a whole step (or two frets). People really tend to play this
kind of change by moving up or down with the chords. As | said before, it’s ok now and then, but don't get in the habit of only
doing that. Play in the key of A minor, and then when it goes down to G minor, try going up toa higher place on the fretboard.
You really need to study the scales up and down the neck to be able to do this. Do you want to play solid, tasteful jazz? Then
you better get busy and start practicing!

100 JAZZ LESSONS | GUITAR LESSON GOLDMINE



Another fairly common change in music involves chords moving down two whole steps, or a major 3rd (F major to D> major,
for example, or A major to F major). Learning to play comfortably over these changes is challenging, but has its rewards.
There's a bulk of Latin music that incorporates this sound. Here again you need to study the keys and their positions in order
to get around on the fretboard fluently. If you have the equipment or program on your computer to record yourself, try
recording or laying down some backing tracks and practice playing along with it or over the changes.

EXAMPLE 3

The ultimate practice sessions are with other people jamming around and experimenting together. As a kid learning jazz.
there was nothing better than discovering some musical know-how with a friend as eager as | was in wanting to get better.

100 JAZZ LESSONS | GUITAR LESSON GOLDMINE 141



MANY SCALES
OVER ONE CHORD

Without a doubt, the most significant musical application in jazz is the use of scales and modes over dominant seven type
chords. The flexibility you have seems limitless. Once that b7th note is introduced in a chord, we have all kinds of options as to
which type of scale or sound we wish to use. A lot of a person’s style and identification will be based on this fact. Joe Pass said
he always tried to use the }5, #5, +9, and #9 notes in all of his improvisations over dominant chords. My question is, “How can he
do it and get away with it?” Dominant seven chords can have all these altered notes added to them (G75, G7#5, G7+9, G7#9,
G7+5:9, etc.). In blues, we hear players using a minor pentatonic scale over a dominant seven chord. We could explain this with
volumes of written materials, but hopefully your ears will discover this reality. These examples have various scales or modes
being used over one type of chord: G7.

MIXOLYDIAN

This can also be thought of as the C major scale over G7.
